text:
Graph RAGs are emerging as a core component of many effective AI agents.
They are not infrastructure. They are enterprise IP.
 
Enterprises have become comfortable outsourcing systems. Data warehouses, ERP platforms, and applications run externally while ownership of data remains internal.
 
But a knowledge graph is different.
 
It doesn't just store data. It defines:
 • how your enterprise is structured
 • how systems relate
 • how decisions impact each other
 
In the context of AI agents, this becomes critical. Agents rely on this structure to interpret data and operate across systems.
 
Over time, the knowledge graph begins to reflect how the enterprise functions.
 
At that point, it becomes more than a technical layer. It becomes enterprise intellectual property.
 
The key question is not where the graph is deployed. It is who defines and controls the structure behind it.
 
Read the full whitepaper: https://lnkd.in/gxSSHTF3
 
#AIAgents #KnowledgeGraphs #GraphRAG #EnterpriseAI #AgenticAI #EnterpriseIP #GenAI

👉 Not all agent workflows are the same.

We tend to say “build an agent” as if there’s only one pattern. There isn’t. Over the past few weeks, I’ve been experimenting with a few:

🔹 **Sequential pipelines**
Step-by-step processing
Extractor → Annotator → Summarizer
Use when each stage depends on the previous one

🔹 **Concurrent (fan-out / fan-in)**
Multiple agents run in parallel → results merged
Use when tasks are independent and latency matters

🔹 **DAG workflows**
Fixed structure, deterministic routing
Use when you need predictability and testability

🔹 **Agent loops (ReAct)**
Model decides what to do next
Use when the path isn’t known upfront

🔹 **Group chat / multi-agent collaboration**
Agents share context and build on each other
Use when ideas need to evolve, not just process


The mistake I see often:

👉 Trying to solve everything with one pattern

But each pattern answers a different question:

* Who decides the next step?
* Is the flow deterministic or emergent?
* Can you predict cost and latency?
* Can you test it?

There’s another dimension to this:

👉 Not just how the flow works…
👉 But who does the work

🔹 **Magentic delegation (agent-as-tool)**
A manager agent calls specialist agents as if they were tools

* keeps control centralized
* isolates responsibilities
* makes specialists reusable

This combines with most orchestration styles — it’s a layering choice, not a replacement


Here’s what helped me make sense of frameworks:

👉 They’re all exposing the same patterns
👉 just in different ways

**Flow (orchestration):**

* LangGraph → explicit graphs (nodes + edges)
* Azure AI Foundry → pipelines / orchestrators
* LlamaIndex → workflows / query pipelines

**Delegation (magentic):**

* LangChain → agents calling tools (including sub-agents)
* Azure → connected agents / tool invocation
* LlamaIndex → tool-using agents / sub-workflows

Different abstractions. Same ideas.

A cleaner way to think about it:

✔ Keep orchestration patterns simple
✔ Add delegation as a second dimension

Orchestration is about **flow**
Magentic is about **who does the work**

They’re not competing ideas. They compose. Most confusion in agent design comes from mixing these two dimensions. Once you separate them, “agent design” becomes much clearer. You’re not choosing a framework. You’re choosing a control model. Frameworks don’t define architecture. They just make certain designs easier.

author.name: Raj Shrinivasan
author.headline: AI Engineer & Builder | Agentic Systems · RAG · LangGraph · Azure AI Foundry | From Idea to Production

text:
Discovery is becoming abundant. Actionability is still scarce.

I recently joined Cyber Defense TV to talk about one of the biggest problems in application security today: the industry is getting better and faster at generating findings, but proving which ones are real is still the harder part.

That changes how security teams work. When a finding cannot be verified, teams end up debating reachability, severity, and priority. A working PoC usually settles that discussion much faster than a long argument ever will.

At ZAST.AI, our approach is built around that reality:
Analyze — combine static analysis, code property graph analysis, and LLM reasoning to understand flows, assets, trust boundaries, and business logic
Generate — produce PoC candidates from the relevant execution context
Verify — run those PoCs against test targets to determine whether the path is actually exploitable

So far, that workflow has helped us turn candidate findings into verified results, contribute 150+ CVEs across projects including Microsoft Azure SDK, Apache Struts, and WordPress, and uncover hundreds of “forever days” in legacy IoT firmware.

One of the main points from the interview was simple: as AI-generated reports continue to multiply, verification becomes more important for everyone in the process — researchers, vendors, CNAs, and defenders trying to prioritize what actually matters.

If you want the full conversation, the episode is here:
🎥 Full episode: https://lnkd.in/gDHy4wTN

#Cybersecurity #AppSec #VulnerabilityResearch #SoftwareSecurity #AIForSecurity

author.name: Geng Yang
author.headline: Founder & CEO at ZAST.AI | Autonomous exploit verification for AppSec teams | From findings to proof

article.title: Zero False Positives: AI Agent Finds Real Vulnerabilities & Generates Exploits | Zast AI
